title: Jamestown is founded
summary: The first permanent English settlement takes root in Virginia.
what: The Virginia Company established Jamestown as a commercial colony.
why: It began the expansion of English settlement and the dispossession of Indigenous peoples along the Atlantic coast.
colle: Can a nation celebrate its origins without hiding colonial violence?

title: Representative government and forced labour in Virginia
summary: Two foundations of colonial America emerge in the same year.
what: Virginia’s first representative assembly met, while the first recorded Africans in English Virginia arrived under coercive conditions.
why: The date captures the contradiction between self-government for some and racial exploitation for others.
colle: Was inequality built into American democracy from the beginning?

title: Declaration of Independence
summary: The colonies proclaim a universal language of rights.
what: The Declaration announced separation from Britain and asserted that all people possess unalienable rights.
why: Its promise of equality remains the yardstick for debates about citizenship, race and justice.
colle: Has America lived up to its founding promise of equality?

title: The Constitution
summary: Federalism and checks and balances become the political framework.
what: Delegates created a stronger federal government divided among executive, legislative and judicial branches.
why: Nearly every modern institutional dispute turns on how this eighteenth-century document should be interpreted.
colle: Are checks and balances still working?

title: The Bill of Rights
summary: Civil liberties are added to the Constitution.
what: The first ten amendments protected freedoms such as speech, religion, due process and the right to bear arms.
why: Their interpretation remains central to arguments about individual liberty and public safety.
colle: Are constitutional rights too difficult to adapt to modern society?

title: The Louisiana Purchase
summary: The country doubles in size.
what: The United States bought a vast territory from France and accelerated westward expansion.
why: Expansion strengthened the republic but intensified Indigenous dispossession and conflict over the spread of slavery.
colle: Can territorial expansion ever be separated from violence?

title: Indian Removal
summary: Federal policy forces Indigenous nations westward.
what: The Indian Removal Act enabled the expulsion of Native peoples from their homelands, culminating in the Trail of Tears.
why: It exposes the gap between democratic ideals and settler colonialism.
colle: How should the United States address the legacy of Indigenous dispossession?

title: The Civil War
summary: The Union survives a war fought over slavery and federal power.
what: Eleven slaveholding states seceded; Union victory preserved the country and destroyed legal slavery.
why: The conflict remains central to disputes over race, states’ rights and national memory.
colle: Why does the Civil War remain politically alive?

title: The Thirteenth Amendment
summary: Slavery is abolished nationwide.
what: The amendment prohibited slavery and involuntary servitude, except as punishment for crime.
why: Abolition ended legal bondage but did not end racial domination or economic exploitation.
colle: Did emancipation create genuine freedom?

title: The Fourteenth Amendment
summary: Citizenship and equal protection enter the Constitution.
what: The amendment defined national citizenship and required states to provide due process and equal protection.
why: It became the constitutional foundation for civil-rights litigation, including school desegregation and marriage equality.
colle: Should courts use the Fourteenth Amendment to expand rights?

title: Industrialisation and mass immigration
summary: The United States becomes an urban industrial power.
what: Factories, railways and corporations expanded while millions of immigrants arrived and labour conflict intensified.
why: The period created enormous wealth but also inequality, segregation and pressure for progressive reform.
colle: Does economic dynamism inevitably produce inequality?

title: The Wall Street Crash
summary: Financial collapse begins the Great Depression.
what: Bank failures, unemployment and falling production devastated households and businesses.
why: The crisis discredited laissez-faire assumptions and expanded expectations of federal responsibility.
colle: How much should government intervene during an economic crisis?

title: The New Deal
summary: Franklin D. Roosevelt expands the federal state.
what: Public works, social insurance, labour protections and financial regulation sought relief, recovery and reform.
why: The New Deal created a lasting debate over whether federal power protects freedom or threatens it.
colle: Did the New Deal save capitalism by reforming it?

title: Pearl Harbor and entry into WWII
summary: The United States enters the Second World War.
what: Japan’s attack on Pearl Harbor brought the country into a global conflict.
why: Wartime mobilisation ended the Depression, expanded federal power and accelerated America’s rise to superpower status.
colle: How did war transform the role of the United States?

title: The atomic bomb and the post-war order
summary: The United States emerges as a global superpower.
what: Atomic bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, and the US helped design institutions such as the United Nations.
why: The moment began the nuclear age and a permanent American leadership role in world affairs.
colle: Can the use of atomic weapons ever be justified?

title: Brown v. Board of Education
summary: The Supreme Court rejects segregated public schools.
what: The Court ruled that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.
why: The decision gave constitutional force to the Civil Rights Movement, though implementation met fierce resistance.
colle: Can court decisions change society without political enforcement?

title: The March on Washington
summary: Martin Luther King Jr delivers “I Have a Dream”.
what: A huge interracial demonstration demanded civil and economic rights.
why: The speech became a moral reference point, but its radical demands for economic justice are often softened in public memory.
colle: Has the dream of racial equality been achieved?

title: The Civil Rights Act
summary: Federal law attacks segregation and discrimination.
what: The Act prohibited discrimination in public accommodations and employment on grounds including race, colour, religion, sex and national origin.
why: It marked a decisive federal intervention against Jim Crow.
colle: Is anti-discrimination law enough to produce equality?

title: The Voting Rights Act
summary: Federal power protects access to the ballot.
what: The Act targeted racial barriers such as literacy tests and authorised federal oversight in jurisdictions with histories of discrimination.
why: Later court decisions and state laws revived debate over how voting rights should be protected.
colle: Is voting access equally protected across the United States?

title: The Moon landing
summary: Apollo 11 places humans on the Moon.
what: Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the lunar surface during the Cold War space race.
why: The achievement symbolised technological ambition, federal investment and American soft power.
colle: Can large public projects still unite a divided nation?

title: Watergate and Nixon’s resignation
summary: A president resigns after abuse of power.
what: Investigations into the Watergate break-in exposed obstruction and misuse of executive authority.
why: The scandal strengthened oversight but also deepened long-term distrust of institutions.
colle: Can democratic institutions effectively restrain a president?

title: The Reagan revolution
summary: Conservatism reshapes national politics.
what: Ronald Reagan promoted tax cuts, deregulation, military strength and a smaller domestic role for government.
why: Reaganism shifted political debate to the right and still shapes arguments about taxes, welfare and national identity.
colle: Did Reagan restore confidence or increase inequality?

title: The 9/11 attacks
summary: Terrorist attacks reshape security and foreign policy.
what: Al-Qaeda attacks killed nearly 3,000 people and prompted wars in Afghanistan and Iraq.
why: The “war on terror” expanded surveillance and executive power while raising enduring questions about civil liberties.
colle: Did the response to 9/11 sacrifice too much liberty for security?

title: Financial crisis and Obama’s election
summary: Economic collapse coincides with a historic election.
what: A severe financial crisis triggered bailouts and recession, while Barack Obama became the first Black president.
why: The year symbolised both racial progress and anger at economic inequality and political elites.
colle: Did Obama’s election mark a post-racial turning point?

title: The Affordable Care Act
summary: The federal government expands health-insurance coverage.
what: “Obamacare” created marketplaces, subsidies and protections for people with pre-existing conditions.
why: Healthcare became a defining ideological battle over rights, markets and federal authority.
colle: Should healthcare be treated as a right?

title: Obergefell v. Hodges
summary: Same-sex marriage becomes a constitutional right.
what: The Supreme Court required all states to license and recognise same-sex marriages.
why: The ruling marked rapid social change and intensified debate over judicial power and religious liberty.
colle: Should fundamental rights depend on court rulings?

title: Pandemic, George Floyd and mass protest
summary: A health crisis and racial reckoning collide.
what: COVID-19 caused mass death and economic disruption, while George Floyd’s murder sparked nationwide Black Lives Matter protests.
why: The year exposed inequalities in health, policing and political trust.
colle: Why did the pandemic deepen America’s political divisions?

title: The Capitol attack
summary: A mob attempts to disrupt the certification of the presidential election.
what: Supporters of Donald Trump stormed the Capitol as Congress counted electoral votes.
why: The attack made election denial and the peaceful transfer of power central tests of American democracy.
colle: How resilient is American democracy when electoral legitimacy is rejected?

title: Dobbs overturns Roe v. Wade
summary: Federal constitutional protection for abortion ends.
what: The Supreme Court returned abortion regulation to elected governments, producing sharply different state laws.
why: The decision intensified the culture wars and highlighted the political power of judicial appointments.
colle: Why does abortion remain such a defining political issue?

title: Donald Trump wins a second, non-consecutive term
summary: Voters return Trump to the White House.
what: Trump defeated Kamala Harris and Republicans also won control of Congress.
why: The result confirmed the strength of populist nationalism and raised new questions about presidential power, institutions and polarisation.
colle: What explains the durability of Trumpism?

title: Trump begins his second presidency
summary: Donald Trump is sworn in as the 47th president.
what: The administration began with an “America First” programme and extensive use of executive action.
why: The return of a former president after defeat and impeachment is unprecedented in modern US politics and tests constitutional norms.
colle: Is the modern presidency becoming too powerful?
